AS 6101B.description: This event completed prior to arrival at the school gives the student the necessary skills using MATLAB for the following modules within the course.

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 6201A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with AS 6202W and AS 6203Z under a shared description block.
AS 6201A.description: This block includes the definition of space, basic physics of space, the natural space environment, the man-made space environment, the physical laws that govern the movement of an orbital body, Kepler's Laws, Newton's Laws, Universal Laws of Gravity, and movement in orbits, solar winds and other effects on space craft. The lecture is followed by a series of case studies of issues/failures of satellites encountered due to the Space Environment related to the environment in practical situations.

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 6210A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with AS 6211E and AS 6212Z under a shared description block.
AS 6210A.description: These lectures cover Inertial Orbits with topics in Orbit Basics, Classical Orbital Elements, Orbit Regimes, Sidereal & Solar Time Comparison, Perturbations, Maneuver Forensics as related to Perturbations, Ground Traces, Orbital Coordinate Frames, and Time Systems. Students will be expected to perform hand calculations related to the topics presented, create their own tools to perform repeated calculations, and will be assigned homework and projects to reinforce concepts presented in block. Students will utilize Systems Tool Kit Software & www.usspacetraining.com as learning aids in visualizing concepts and for use in assigned projects.

AS 6410A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with AS 6411L under a shared description block.
AS 6410A.description: This exercise is the integration event for Estimation and Kalman Filtering. Students will be given a scenario to evaluate the relative navigation system with multiple Kalman filter settings for a new satellite capable of rendezvous and proximity operations. Students will be given a test plan and measures to execute within the WebTools simulator and evaluate their system using a series of pre-planned test maneuvers. Students must apply knowledge gained during the Test Foundations series to plan, execute, analyze, and report on test results. After executing a series of test points, the test team will have to mission plan a new test point to utilize the remaining available velocity of the satellite within the simulator to get a more complete data set on one of the Kalman filters. Upon completion of their test points, students will complete and turn in a limited scope report based on the test data.

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 6601A.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Control for Domain Sciences Lecture

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 6601A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with AS 6602E and AS 6603Z under a shared description block. Taught consecutively with FQ 7300 Data-Driven Control.
AS 6601A.description: This block will cover advanced Data-Driven control techniques. It includes a broad scope of topics that build an understanding of aspects to consider when testing an autonomy-based system.  Topics include identifying and analyzing different types of control systems and when to apply them, describing different categories of data and proper methods for their analysis, describing different forms and fidelity of simulation and their application, analyzing the use of Monte Carlo testing to include its advantages and pitfalls, and identifying key issues to consider when building up to and executing flight tests. It is taught consecutively with FQ 7300 Data-Driven Control.

AS 7110A.eventName: Satellite Attitude Dynamics, Estimation, and Control Lecture

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 7110A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with AS 7111E, AS 7112L, and AS 7113Z under a shared description block. MCG prereq line cites 'AS 7101A Satellite Systems Review' — drifts from event's official name 'AS 7101A Satellite Systems Review Lecture'; preserved verbatim as written.
AS 7110A.description: These blocks elaborate on nonlinear (and linearized) motion state dynamics (including autogenetic gravity center location); nonlinear state estimation; and nonlinear and linear state control (e.g. disturbance rejection). Lectures include time propagation of Euler's coupled, nonlinear moment equations relative to inertial reference frames and expressed in coordinates of the body reference frame. Luenberger observers are introduced as one option for nonlinear state estimation. Nonlinear feedforward control is introduced with dynamic inversion for feedback, optionally augmented by linear state feedback control. Structural dynamics are taught in the context of controlling highly flexible space robotics with hands-on elaboration on the robotic on-orbit trainer during AS 7140S GROOT Handling Qualities. These lectures can take place before or after AS 7150O. The lab provides additional details into attitude estimation and control design considerations, reference frames, Euler angles & quaternions, coordinate frame rotations, attitude sensor types / design, and an introduction to attitude control. Students will be expected to perform hand calculations related to the topics presented and create their own tools to perform repeated calculations. This lab provides hands-on experience using the Aerocube satellite system of the material learned in AS 7110A Satellite Attitude Estimation & Control.

AS 7120A.description: This lecture provides additional details into Power Subsystem architecture, details on solar panels, battery design, power distribution, conditioning & fusing, fault design, and power balance analysis. Students will work on a project with a provided excel tool for determining operations design to ensure positive energy balance for a CubeSat.

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 7130L.description: Students will conduct multiple lab exercises using the EyasSat small satellite model aligned to the various satellite subsystems including the EPS, Structure, GNC and CDH. Basic tests will be conducted to familiarize students with the subsystems and determine if the EyasSat meets specified requirements.

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 7140S.eventNotes: MCG lists four additional lecture prereqs (TF 6102A, TF 6201A, TF 6320A, TF 6401A) immediately before the AS 7150O section header on p.51-52, appearing to continue the AS 7140S prereq block. Their placement is ambiguous — they are captured here as additional prereqs but may alternatively belong to AS 7150O. Raw MCG text for the ambiguous block (p.52 lines 395-398): 'TF 6102A Test Planning Lecture / TF 6201A Test Conduct Lecture / TF 6320A Data Presentation Lecture / TF 6401A Test Reporting Lecture'.
AS 7140S.description: Students will assess the handling qualities of the GROOT satellite in a variety of configurations and analyze the platform's stability in translational and rotational maneuver. Students will assess the handling qualities of the GROOT satellite including ability to perform the mission it was designed for. This exercise will require students to apply knowledge gained during the Test Foundations series to plan, execute, analyze, and report on their test results.

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 7150O.description: Students will plan a series of passes to assess the Aerocube ADCS. Students will use their test results to develop an updated moment of inertia matrix for the satellite.

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 7160A.description: Students will learn loads analysis for single degree of freedom systems; attitude trajectories; issues we create; deterministic artificial intelligence; exact linear parameterization vs. linearization; mass moments of inertia (matrix/tensor); feedback control and observers: and linear and nonlinear motion state observers. A 2-body mini-Groot example will be offered leading to understandings of structural filtering and culminating in a computation lab assignment: an n-body mini-Groot control with Bandpass + Notch and trajectory shaping. The culminating hands–on activity includes application to GROOT.

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 7212Z.description: This block provides an overview of typical initial satellite testing after launch, with examples of failures, setting priorities for test to minimize probability of failure, insertion orbit considerations, timelines and challenges to timelines like space weather.

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 7301A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with AS 7302L, AS 7303E, and AS 7304Z under a shared description block. MCG prereq line cites 'AS 7101A Satellite Systems Review' — drifts from event's official name; preserved verbatim.
AS 7301A.description: This lecture describes propulsion basics, including the thrust equation, specific impulse and efficiency, the basic properties of a rocket nozzle, solid fueled rockets, liquid propellant rockets, advantages and disadvantages of different fuels, and briefly discusses some rocket testing requirements. Additionally, on-orbit propulsion systems, including function, limitations, and applications will be discussed. Concepts are reinforced with a tour of the AFRL rocket test facilities at Edwards AFB. Students will develop background knowledge to perform basic calculations of rocket/thruster performance.

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 8301A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with AS 8302E and AS 8303Z under a shared description block.
AS 8301A.description: This course will teach students advanced Kalman Filtering and relative navigation for space applications. Students will develop an understanding between sensor performance measures and Kalman filter performance with appropriate test measures. The students will also gain an understanding of how RPO profiles and maneuvers affect state estimates and ability to assess a Rel Nav system. Students will gain hands on experience with the simulator on www.usspacetraining.com to demonstrate key concepts and prepare them for estimation testing and the subsequent estimation exercise

AS 8310A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with AS 8311E under a shared description block.
AS 8310A.description: The students will be given a test plan and measures to execute within the Webtools simulator. Students will evaluate their system in the simulator using a series of pre-planned test maneuvers. This exercise will also require students to apply knowledge gained during the Test Foundations series to plan, execute, analyze, and report on their test results. After executing a series of test points, the test team will have to mission plan a new test point to utilize the remaining delta-v on the satellite within the simulator to get a more complete data set on one of the Kalman filters. Upon completion of their test points, students will complete and turn in a limited scope report based on the test data. This exercise is the integration event for Estimation and Kalman filtering. Students will be given a scenario to evaluate the relative navigation system w/multiple Kalman filter settings for a new RPO-capable satellite.

prerequisites.name: Advanced Kalman Filter and Relative Navigation Lecture

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 9201A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with AS 9202E and AS 9203Z under a shared description block.
AS 9201A.description: Lectures cover the five major threat threads of Co-orbital Anti-Satellites (ASAT), direct ascent ASAT, Directed Energy Weapons (DEW), Cyber Attack, and Ground Infrastructure Attack. This course will consist of a series of classified lectures and will discuss adversary threats with the potential to restrict US freedom of action in space and disrupt, deny, degrade, and destroy space assets providing critical capabilities to the warfighter. Synthesis event where students will perform a threat surface analysis of a space system against a specific threat thread and brief it to the block. Students will be assessed on their threat analysis and presentation.

AS 9901A.eventName: Space Shuttle Proximity Maneuvering - Guest Lecture

applicabilityDetail.description: Space Test Course only (Astronautical Sciences Phase is STC Only per MCG p.42)

AS 9901A.description: This lecture will be taught in an unclassified setting as a guest lecture and will provide a description of the rendezvous process and issues associated with close proximity maneuvering with another orbiting object. This includes the description of the space shuttle aircraft and MIR, the role of the space shuttle pilot in rendezvous and close proximity maneuvering, and provides essential lessons learned from extended close proximity operations.

CF 6301G.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with CF 6302A, CF 6303S, CF 6304S, CF 6305S under a shared description block.
CF 6301G.description: The T-38 ground school will include an introduction to the T-38 check flight phase and instruction on T-38 systems to include electrical abnormalities, avionics, engines, flight controls, and hydraulics. Training covers safety equipment and egress procedures using either a training device or aircraft. The purpose of cockpit familiarization and procedures training is to familiarize the student with T-38 mission preparation, aircraft procedures, operations limits, and emergency procedures. Training occurs at the aircraft.

CF 6620B.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with CF 6621C and CF 6622C under a shared description block.
CF 6620B.description: The asynchronous block introduces students to basic control room operations and presents the IADS® analysis software to the students. In addition to providing principles for good control room screen design, the block introduces the major capabilities of the IADS software as well as techniques to confirm proper system operation prior to mission start. Students will learn how to create real-time analysis windows in IADS, how to display data effectively, how to insert event markers and use test points to take notes, and how to use inherent IADS capabilities to streamline real-time execution. The objective of the Control Room Familiarization is to introduce the student to control room operations in a mission playback environment. The control room observation can be conducted along with any other curriculum control room event using live aircraft.

FQ 6101A.description: The rigid body equations of motion for an aircraft are derived, and then trim solutions are presented. The equations are linearized in both state space as well as transfer function methods.

FQ 6201A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 6202E and FQ 6203Z under a shared description block.
FQ 6201A.description: The concepts of static and dynamic stability are applied to aircraft. Aircraft modes of motion are introduced. Stability derivatives and their influence are presented. Finally, MIL-STD 1797B guidelines are introduced and applied to the static and dynamic stability parameters to predict an aircraft's handling qualities or to assess a specification compliance. Concepts taught in this course are reinforced using a simulation laboratory exercise.

FQ 6301A.eventName: T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Brief

applicabilityDetail.description: All students — T-38 flying qualities test plan brief

FQ 6301A.description: This event introduces the students to the flying qualities T-38 test plan. It will introduce the plan in three parts. The first part is longitudinal where students are left with constructing cards from given test objectives and measures of performance. The second part is lateral-directional where students will develop measures of performance and cards from given objectives. The third part will be conducted later after handling qualities academics. In that phase students will have to determine objectives, measures of performance and develop cards from a set of requirements. In all parts, students will be responsible for defending their test approaches and mitigation/build-up plans to a reviewing T38 IP and IFTE.

FQ 6320W.eventName: T-38 Flying Qualities Test Plan Working Group

applicabilityDetail.description: All students — T-38 FQ test plan working group

FQ 6320W.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 6321R under a shared description block. Event type suffix 'W' (Working Group) not in base knowledge-base suffix table; treated as its own type.
FQ 6320W.description: This a project composes several events, all tightly integrated to guide the student along its progression. The student will explore MIL-STD-1797 while collecting data in an unsupervised fashion. It will give the student the opportunity to participate in planning a test across multiple flights while ensuring the quality of the data collected is high enough to write a report detailing the findings. The student will develop measures of performance and test maneuvers to assess the open loop flying qualities of the T-38 as an area defense fighter. Not only does this event illustrate how flying qualities or spec-compliance testing might happen, but it also enforces requirements traceability and associates test maneuvers with test objectives via measures of performance. All the student's testing experience to this point will be required to make a final recommendation as to whether to purchase this aircraft for a developing nation.

FQ 7101A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 7102E and FQ 7103Z under a shared description block.
FQ 7101A.description: Feedback flight control systems are introduced along with motivation for transition from mechanical to automatic flight control systems. Linear analysis techniques are presented with applications to aircraft flight controls. These fundamental concepts will be used throughout the 7000-level course. Feedback flight control system implementation is introduced to demonstrate how augmented control systems are used to enhance air vehicle stability and performance. Examples of classical proportional, integral, derivative and LQR (linear quadratic regulator) control systems are presented as is gain scheduling, mode shaping. Basic linear control design and analysis techniques are then applied to aircraft. Considerations for modern aircraft with relevant examples are given as well as issues associated with flight testing control systems. Finally, an introduction to digital control and other analysis planes are discussed.

FQ 7201A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 7202A, FQ 7203E, and FQ 7204Z under a shared description block.
FQ 7201A.description: Static structural analysis is presented along with basic stress/strain analysis. Dynamics applied to non-rigid bodies is also presented, to include basic dynamics and ending with flutter and aeroservoelasticity. A fundamental exposition of basic analysis techniques used for aircraft structures is reviewed. Statics and dynamic structural test. Techniques are presented and include both ground and flight test considerations and buildup.

FQ 7301A.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Control for Domain Sciences Lecture
FQ 7301A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 7302E and FQ 7303Z under a shared description block.
FQ 7301A.description: This block will cover advanced Data-Driven control techniques. It includes a broad scope of topics that build an understanding of aspects to consider when testing an autonomy-based system.  Topics include identifying and analyzing different types of control systems and when to apply them, describing different categories of data and proper methods for their analysis, describing different forms and fidelity of simulation and their application, analyzing the use of Monte Carlo testing to include its advantages and pitfalls, and identifying key issues to consider when building up to and executing flight tests. It is taught consecutively with AS 6600 Data-Driven Control.

FQ 8210Y.eventName: T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan Oral Report

applicabilityDetail.description: All students — T-38 handling qualities test plan oral report

FQ 8210Y.description: This project is a continuation of the FQ 6301A Flying Qualities Test Plan Project. Building on the knowledge the student gained from that project, they will first design handling qualities tasks to evaluate the mission performance of the T-38 as an area defense fighter for foreign military sales. The student will be briefed in the FQ 8201M T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan MIB where expectations will be set and requirements for the project will be discussed. Next they will design tasks and present test cards to be signed by a T-38 Instructor Pilot. The student will then go execute their plan to gather data in FQ 8202F T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan Data Flight. Once you have collected and analyzed all your data, you will present your findings to a board in the FQ 8210Y T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan Oral Report.

FQ 9201A.eventName: Stall, Departure and Spin Testing Lecture
FQ 9201A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 9202E and FQ 9203Z under a shared description block.
FQ 9201A.description: The aerodynamics associated with stall are presented and various high-angle-of-attack terms are formally defined. The equations of motion are revisited for coupling and spins and then analyzed. High angle-of- attack test methodologies are presented including those discussed by MIL-STD-1797B and MIL-F-83691B. Analysis and recovery techniques for this regime of flight are developed for use in future flight tests.

FQ 9240M.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 9241W, FQ 9242W, FQ 9243W, FQ 9244S, FQ 9245C, FQ 9246F, FQ 9247C, and FQ 9248Y under a shared description block. MCG prereq list includes alternative prereqs joined by 'or' (FQ 6252C or TF 6231C); both captured.
FQ 9240M.description: For the High AOA Envelope Expansion project, the student will receive project requirements and be given information needed to complete the project at the MIB. The students will then be given time to develop a plan during the instructor facilitated FQ 9241W F-16 High AOA Envelope Expansion TPWG 1. The students will then be broken up into teams where they will finalize their plan during FQ 9242W F-16 High AOA Envelope Expansion TPWG 2 and FQ 9243W High AOA Envelope Expansion Flight Readiness Review. The students will demonstrate to their instructors that they can execute their plan in the rehearsal sim and control room. Finally, the teams will execute their plan on the data flight and control room. The team will analyze and report on their data as part of FQ 9248Y F-16 High AOA Envelope Expansion Oral Report.

FQ 9241W.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 9240M, FQ 9242W, FQ 9243W, FQ 9244S, FQ 9245C, FQ 9246F, FQ 9247C, and FQ 9248Y under a shared description block.
FQ 9241W.description: For the High AOA Envelope Expansion project, the student will receive project requirements and be given information needed to complete the project at the MIB. The students will then be given time to develop a plan during the instructor facilitated FQ 9241W F-16 High AOA Envelope Expansion TPWG 1. The students will then be broken up into teams where they will finalize their plan during FQ 9242W F-16 High AOA Envelope Expansion TPWG 2 and FQ 9243W High AOA Envelope Expansion Flight Readiness Review. The students will demonstrate to their instructors that they can execute their plan in the rehearsal sim and control room. Finally, the teams will execute their plan on the data flight and control room. The team will analyze and report on their data as part of FQ 9248Y F-16 High AOA Envelope Expansion Oral Report.

FQ 9301A.description: The analytical process and flight test methods used to qualify stores on aircraft for safe operation are introduced.

FQ 9401A.description: This block introduces the concept of aircraft flight envelope expansion as well as the creation of aircraft flight manuals using testing that will occur during the Flying Qualities Phase.

FQ 9410A.eventName: First Flight and Envelope Expansion Lecture
FQ 9410A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 9411E and FQ 9412Z under a shared description block.
FQ 9410A.description: The process leading to first flight of an aircraft is presented. Issues associated with experimental flight test are presented.

FQ 9411E.eventName: First Flight and Envelope Expansion Exam
FQ 9411E.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 9410A and FQ 9412Z under a shared description block.
FQ 9411E.description: The process leading to first flight of an aircraft is presented. Issues associated with experimental flight test are presented.

FQ 9412Z.eventName: First Flight and Envelope Expansion Debrief
FQ 9412Z.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 9410A and FQ 9411E under a shared description block.
FQ 9412Z.description: The process leading to first flight of an aircraft is presented. Issues associated with experimental flight test are presented.

FQ 9501M.eventName: Sim Flying Qualities Practical Exam MIB (ABM/CSO/RPA)

applicabilityDetail.description: ABM/CSO/RPA — sim flying qualities practical exam MIB (goal-event series MIB)

FQ 9501M.eventNotes: Introductory MIB for FQ 9502S goal-event series. MCG lists FQ 9501M on its own line above the grouped FQ 9502S / FQ 9503R description block; no explicit prereqs listed for FQ 9501M.

applicabilityDetail.description: ABM/CSO/RPA goal event — sim flying qualities practical exam

FQ 9502S.eventNotes: Goal event for ABM/CSO/RPA. Grouped in MCG with FQ 9503R under a shared description block.
FQ 9502S.description: The objective of the sim is to evaluate the student ABM/CSO/RPA ability to plan, execute, and report a flying and handling qualities evaluation using hand-held data techniques. The students are given a scenario in which they must conduct an initial evaluation of a proposed solution to address the significant time delays often encountered when flying RPAs or handling remotely guided munitions. The student is tasked to plan and fly the sim then provide an informal written report on the results and recommendations.

prerequisites.name: T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan Oral Report

FQ 9503R.eventName: Sim Flying Qualities Practical Exam Written Report (ABM/CSO/RPA)

applicabilityDetail.description: ABM/CSO/RPA — sim flying qualities practical exam written report

FQ 9503R.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with FQ 9502S under a shared description block.
FQ 9503R.description: The objective of the sim is to evaluate the student ABM/CSO/RPA ability to plan, execute, and report a flying and handling qualities evaluation using hand-held data techniques. The students are given a scenario in which they must conduct an initial evaluation of a proposed solution to address the significant time delays often encountered when flying RPAs or handling remotely guided munitions. The student is tasked to plan and fly the sim then provide an informal written report on the results and recommendations.

prerequisites.name: T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan Oral Report

FQ 9510M.eventName: T-38 Flying Qualities Practical Exam MIB (FTE/P)

applicabilityDetail.description: FTE/P — T-38 flying qualities practical exam MIB

FQ 9510M.eventNotes: Introductory MIB for FQ 9511F / FQ 9512C goal-event pair. Grouped in MCG with FQ 9511F, FQ 9512C, and FQ 9513R under a shared description block.
FQ 9510M.description: The objective of the sortie is to evaluate the student pilot/FTE ability to plan, execute, and report a flying and handling qualities evaluation. The students are given a scenario in which information on the T-38 pitch PIO susceptibility at high speed and low altitude is requested. It will take more than one sortie to provide this information, so an engineering team elects to fly a single sortie to narrow the focus of the effort. The students are tasked to plan and fly this sortie then provide an informal written report to the engineering team on the results and recommendations for worthwhile additional testing.

prerequisites.name: T-38 Handling Qualities Test Plan Oral Report

applicabilityDetail.description: P goal event — T-38 flying qualities practical exam (flight side of paired practical)

PF 5101A.description: Basic overview of the four performance data group test plans as well as the reports that are due following the flight data collection: Air Data System Calibration Report, Model Validation Report, and Performance Phase Final Test Report.

PF 6101A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 6102E and PF 6103Z under a shared description block.
PF 6101A.description: Subsonic aerodynamic fundamentals, equations and terminology used throughout the USAF TPS curriculum; airfoil analysis, forces acting on the aircraft, pressure gradients and the Bernoulli equation, and drag coefficients; boundary layer separation and control; Mach and Reynolds numbers and pitching moments.

PF 6102E.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 6101A and PF 6103Z under a shared description block.
PF 6102E.description: Subsonic aerodynamic fundamentals, equations and terminology used throughout the USAF TPS curriculum; airfoil analysis, forces acting on the aircraft, pressure gradients and the Bernoulli equation, and drag coefficients; boundary layer separation and control; Mach and Reynolds numbers and pitching moments.

PF 6103Z.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 6101A and PF 6102E under a shared description block.
PF 6103Z.description: Subsonic aerodynamic fundamentals, equations and terminology used throughout the USAF TPS curriculum; airfoil analysis, forces acting on the aircraft, pressure gradients and the Bernoulli equation, and drag coefficients; boundary layer separation and control; Mach and Reynolds numbers and pitching moments.

PF 6110A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 6111E and PF 6112Z under a shared description block.
PF 6110A.description: Compressible flow from transonic to hypersonic; shock waves; boundary layer control over supersonic wings; airfoil shapes, shapes of aircraft structures, and transitions from subsonic to supersonic flight; lift and drag approximations for supersonic airfoil sections; principles of hypersonic aerodynamics and aerothermodynamics and their effect on aerospace vehicles.

PF 6111E.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 6110A and PF 6112Z under a shared description block.
PF 6111E.description: Compressible flow from transonic to hypersonic; shock waves; boundary layer control over supersonic wings; airfoil shapes, shapes of aircraft structures, and transitions from subsonic to supersonic flight; lift and drag approximations for supersonic airfoil sections; principles of hypersonic aerodynamics and aerothermodynamics and their effect on aerospace vehicles.

PF 6112Z.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 6110A and PF 6111E under a shared description block.
PF 6112Z.description: Compressible flow from transonic to hypersonic; shock waves; boundary layer control over supersonic wings; airfoil shapes, shapes of aircraft structures, and transitions from subsonic to supersonic flight; lift and drag approximations for supersonic airfoil sections; principles of hypersonic aerodynamics and aerothermodynamics and their effect on aerospace vehicles.

PF 6120M.eventNotes: MIB appears immediately before PF 6121F in MCG with no dedicated prerequisites list. No description block.

applicabilityDetail.description: All students — flown in T-38 as airborne simulator for low L/D approach/landing

PF 6121F.description: The objective of this mission is to familiarize the student with low L/D approach and landing flight characteristics using the T-38C aircraft as an airborne simulator. The profiles flown simulate low L/D landing pattern approaches for typical unpowered research aircraft such as the X-24B and the Space Shuttle.

applicabilityDetail.description: FTC + STC (all standard students; ungraded for STC)

PF 6201A.eventNotes: Tagged (FTC/STC) in MCG — applies to both standard (FTC) and space (STC) tracks. Grouped with PF 6202E and PF 6203Z under a shared description block. Course is ungraded for STC.
PF 6201A.description: Use of analysis, Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), and Wind Tunnel testing to predict aircraft performance. Programmatic considerations for use of Modeling and Simulation (M&S). Theory for the development, validation, and use of lift curves and drag polars during flight testing of aerospace vehicles, discussion of steady state, quasi-steady state, and dynamic methods. Determining uncertainty and interpreting its effect on data. This course is ungraded for STC.

PF 6202E.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 6201A and PF 6203Z under a shared description block.
PF 6202E.description: Use of analysis, Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), and Wind Tunnel testing to predict aircraft performance. Programmatic considerations for use of Modeling and Simulation (M&S). Theory for the development, validation, and use of lift curves and drag polars during flight testing of aerospace vehicles, discussion of steady state, quasi-steady state, and dynamic methods. Determining uncertainty and interpreting its effect on data. This course is ungraded for STC.

PF 6203Z.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 6201A and PF 6202E under a shared description block.
PF 6203Z.description: Use of analysis, Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), and Wind Tunnel testing to predict aircraft performance. Programmatic considerations for use of Modeling and Simulation (M&S). Theory for the development, validation, and use of lift curves and drag polars during flight testing of aerospace vehicles, discussion of steady state, quasi-steady state, and dynamic methods. Determining uncertainty and interpreting its effect on data. This course is ungraded for STC.

PF 6210A.description: Reasons for standardizing data to standard conditions; defensible methods for correcting flight test data from test conditions to standard conditions.

PF 6220M.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 6221F under a shared description block.
PF 6220M.description: CSO/P/RPA will receive a flight in the T-38 to practice the test methods used to collect lift and drag data at constant Mach numbers through stabilized and dynamic flight maneuvers.

PF 6221F.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 6220M under a shared description block.
PF 6221F.description: CSO/P/RPA will receive a flight in the T-38 to practice the test methods used to collect lift and drag data at constant Mach numbers through stabilized and dynamic flight maneuvers.

applicabilityDetail.description: All students — flown in each student's data group aircraft

PF 6230F.eventNotes: Data-group-branching flight: each student flies their data group aircraft. CF prereqs are DG-conditioned per bracket annotation.
PF 6230F.description: All students will fly a data collection flight to gather data for their model validation report using their data group aircraft.

Learjet CF-2 (ABM/CSO/RPA) [req'd for Learjet Data Group]
C-12 CF-3 (P, crew solo) [req'd for C-12 Data Group]
C-12 Flight Training (ABM/CSO/FTE) [req'd for C-12 Data Group]
Control Room Familiarization [req'd for Learjet Data Group]
F-16 CF-4 (P, crew solo) [req'd for F-16 Data Group]
F-16 Flight Training (ABM/CSO/FTE/RPA) [req'd for F-16 Data Group]

PF 6240A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 6241R under a shared description block.

PF 7101A.description: Properties of the earth's atmosphere applicable to flight testing; definition of the U.S. Standard Atmosphere and its assumptions; Pitot-static instrumentation; all inputs, outputs and limitations associated with Pitot-static systems including the procedures for error computation and correction and conversion of test readings to U.S. Standard Day calibrations; reduction of test data from tower fly-bys to determine the position errors inherent in aircraft on-board Pitot-static systems.

PF 7201A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 7202E and PF 7203Z under a shared description block.
PF 7201A.description: These blocks cover the fundamentals of propulsion with an emphasis on flight test applications. It begins with first-principles analysis and applies them to various propulsion types, with a primary focus on turbine engines. Key topics include engine components, cycle analysis, performance evaluation, operability, and control characteristics. Students will assess propulsion system limitations, failure modes, and advanced concepts, including emerging technologies and alternative propulsion methods. The course prepares students to analyze, evaluate, and test propulsion systems in a flight test environment using analytical methods and practical applications.

PF 7202E.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 7201A and PF 7203Z under a shared description block.

PF 8101A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 8102M under a shared description block.
PF 8101A.description: Equations that can be used to determine takeoff performance and to correct flight test data to standard conditions; equations to analyze ground roll and speed on landing, braking distances and the effects of reverse thrust, aerodynamic braking and runway surface conditions on landing; effects of atmospheric influences and variations in pilot technique at varying gross weights under various conditions; discussion of a variety of typical takeoff and landing tests accomplished at AFTC; braking, anti-skid and barrier testing. The brief will cover test techniques for performing takeoff and landing tests and gathering data for takeoff and landing ground roll distances; development of a method for reducing these data to standard day conditions; and safety considerations that apply. Though specific flights are not dedicated to collecting takeoff and landing data, it is expected that students will collect the data on all applicable sorties.

PF 8102M.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 8101A under a shared description block.
PF 8102M.description: Equations that can be used to determine takeoff performance and to correct flight test data to standard conditions; equations to analyze ground roll and speed on landing, braking distances and the effects of reverse thrust, aerodynamic braking and runway surface conditions on landing; effects of atmospheric influences and variations in pilot technique at varying gross weights under various conditions; discussion of a variety of typical takeoff and landing tests accomplished at AFTC; braking, anti-skid and barrier testing. The brief will cover test techniques for performing takeoff and landing tests and gathering data for takeoff and landing ground roll distances; development of a method for reducing these data to standard day conditions; and safety considerations that apply. Though specific flights are not dedicated to collecting takeoff and landing data, it is expected that students will collect the data on all applicable sorties.

PF 8110A.description: Theoretical background required to determine aircraft cruise performance; data reduction methods oriented toward providing cruise data in the format used at the AFTC; definition of the basic cruise assumption of level unaccelerated flight; development of lift and drag functional relationships based on subsonic aerodynamics in a form suitable for cruise performance analysis; determination of thrust and fuel flow parameters; derivation of the equations for range and endurance; determination of optimum fuel flows and RPM settings for cruise.

PF 8201A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 8202E and PF 8203Z under a shared description block.
PF 8201A.description: Relationships of various forces acting on an aircraft in flight; the simplification of equations for steady climb and descent; factors that affect optimum angle of climb, maximum rate of climb and time to climb; analysis of polar diagrams for energy management; analysis of the forces acting on an aircraft in a turn; factors affecting turn performance; computation of corrections to standard day; methods of testing turn performance for non-standard day atmospheric and non-standard weight conditions; derivation and explanation of the equations used to correct check climb, level acceleration, and turn performance data.

PF 8202E.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 8201A and PF 8203Z under a shared description block.
PF 8202E.description: Relationships of various forces acting on an aircraft in flight; the simplification of equations for steady climb and descent; factors that affect optimum angle of climb, maximum rate of climb and time to climb; analysis of polar diagrams for energy management; analysis of the forces acting on an aircraft in a turn; factors affecting turn performance; computation of corrections to standard day; methods of testing turn performance for non-standard day atmospheric and non-standard weight conditions; derivation and explanation of the equations used to correct check climb, level acceleration, and turn performance data.

PF 8203Z.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with PF 8201A and PF 8202E under a shared description block.

SO 6101A.description: An overview of the GROOT system and its operation are provided. Maneuver academics introduces the student to mission planning, briefing, execution, and debrief (PBED) of two satellites operating near each other to students with limited satellite operations and rendezvous and proximity operations (RPO) experience. Robotics academics reinforces PBED and continues to build basic satellite ops knowledge and skills.

SO 6201A.description: Students are introduced to roles and responsibilities, battle rhythm, flight safety and best practices for Aerocube satellite operations.

SO 6210A.description: Students are introduced to the Aerocube space segment, satellite modes, vehicle commanding, telemetry, orbit, and attitude. Students are also provided an overview of the Aerocube ground segment, stations and tools

SO 6220H.description: This event is designed to introduce students to the display layout for Aerocube operations. Students will learn how to configure the operator and crew commander terminals and discuss how each element supports operations.

SO 6230E.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SO 6231O under a shared description block. MCG prereq line cites 'SO 6220H Aerocube Display Familiarization' — drifts from event's official name 'SO 6220H SOCR Display Familiarization'; preserved verbatim as written.
SO 6230E.description: Students will complete an examination to configure the display and explain operational relevance of each window. Scheduled concurrently with SO 6231O Contact Demo. Students will learn to build commands, pass plans, and how to run a nominal Aerocube satellite contact. Students must pass their exam to proceed into the contact demo

SY 5101A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 5102L and SY 5103R under a shared description block.
SY 5101A.description: This 4-hour academic block is an overview of the essential math, physics, and engineering knowledge that span many mission systems. The lab introduces the student to test methodologies for evaluating integrated avionics systems in the F-16 UTD. The student is exposed to an F-16 Operational Flight Program (OFP) with a deficiency, enabled to learn about the OFP update process, and required to evaluate the OFP to perform limited regression testing to determine if new deficiencies were inadvertently created.

SY 5102L.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 5101A and SY 5103R under a shared description block.

SY 5201A.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Decision Making for Mission Systems Lecture
SY 5201A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 5202E and SY 5203Z under a shared description block.
SY 5201A.description: This block will cover data-driven testing for mission systems, including a broad scope of topics that include data science, uncertainty quantification, test & evaluation, statistical hypothesis testing, and decision making. Topics covered in the 1-week block include exploratory data analysis, data visualization, structured vs unstructured data, streaming vs. post-mission data, meta-data tagging, and structured inputs and data pipelines. It will also cover key performance indicators (KPIs), system performance and predictive modelling, validation and verification in data-driven test, statistical comparisons, Experimental design, adversarial machine learning & resilience strategies, and software for T&E of data-driven mission systems. Additionally, the block will cover probabilistic reasoning, reinforcement learning, autonomous control with RL (model-free and model-based), ethical considerations, data and decision fusion techniques, and considerations for tactical vs. operational vs. strategic environments.

SY 6101A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 6102E, SY 6103Z, and SY 6104A under a shared description block.
SY 6101A.description: This 9-hour academic lecture course provides students with the theoretical background necessary to understand how ultraviolet, visible, and infrared electromagnetic energy is used in military applications. Several fundamental types of EO systems are presented, and their specific applications are discussed. Measures of performance and figures of merit for various systems are presented as well as specific ground and flight test methodologies used to evaluate performance.

SY 6110A.description: This 8-hour academic lecture course builds upon the Mission Systems Fundamentals lecture and provides the academic knowledge necessary to apply those fundamentals to development and test of military RF sensors. SY 6120L.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 6121S and SY 6122S under a shared description block.
SY 6120L.description: These blocks build on the RADAR academic knowledge with a hands-on laboratory and one hands-on simulator (6121S or 6122S). The first RADAR lab uses the TPS LabVolt laboratory RADAR to demonstrate theoretical RADAR/test concepts as well as basic RADAR displays (A-Scope, and PPI). The RADAR simulator occurs in the F-16 UTD for uncleared students, and in the F-22 Software Integration Lab (SIL) for cleared students. Students learn basic and advanced RADAR test techniques for A/A and A/G RADAR.

SY 6131F.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 6132R under a shared description block.
SY 6131F.description: This mission is designed to introduce the student to the differences between visible (TV), infrared (IR), and ground mapping RADAR sensors in the flight environment and to introduce several flight test techniques to evaluate the sensors both qualitatively and quantitatively. Knowledge gained during the EO ground test will be applied to operate a Targeting Pod in an inflight environment. The Sensors Flight provides data to support a written report due 5 business days after the last team sortie.

SY 6201A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 6202L, SY 6203I, and SY 6204R under a shared description block.
SY 6201A.description: This lecture covers RF and Communications systems fundamentals to prepare the students for the Communications and RF exercise. This lecture series will discuss the basics of data encoding, the link equation, antenna fundamentals, modulation techniques and multiple access techniques. The hands-on lab demonstrating RF and Communications systems fundamentals will be executed in combination with the Space Communications lecture. It will prepare the students for the Communications and RF exercise. The students will also gain familiarization with the software defined radio program, Hack RF equipment, and antenna connectivity. The students will gain familiarization with communications and antenna measures and measurement techniques. The demonstrations will step the students through a build-up of activities including RF Fundamentals using Software Defined Radios, Antennas and Common RF sources; Polarization; Signal interference and EW Measures; Antenna Patterns: Mainlobes / Sidelobes. The exercise is the integration event for Communications and RF instructions. The students will be presented a communications and RF scenario. They will work in small teams to develop the relevant sections of a detailed test plan which they will submit as a deliverable. They will then execute their approved test plan, collect relevant communications, and RF data, analyze their results, and submit a limited evaluation as a final graded assessment. The scope is estimated to be one afternoon of scenario presentation, RF lab equipment issue, Q&A, and deliverables overview. Two afternoons are scheduled for test plan writing and one afternoon for feedback on Test Plan and corrections. Two afternoons are slated for test execution, data collection and analysis. Two afternoons are scheduled for writing of the final assessment against the mission requirements.

SY 6301A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 6302E, SY 6303Y, SY 6304Y, and SY 6305Z under a shared description block.
SY 6301A.description: This 8-hour academic lecture Phase introduces aircraft navigation systems, including basic theory of operation, positioning information characteristics and basic test methodology. Topics include the basic principles of navigating across earth's surface, geodetic reference frames, ground-based radio navigation, Coriolis acceleration, inertial navigation, space-based satellite navigation with special emphasis on Global Positioning System applications, and integrated navigation and guidance systems using Kalman filtering techniques. The emphasis here is on understanding the fundamental principles of operation, what information is provided to the integrated navigation solution of the platform, and the limitations and potentials of these systems as they apply to systems testing and evaluation. There is also considerable discussion of the errors and analysis of large sets of navigation data. Students will be assigned groups to analyze example navigation performance data provided by the instructor in the form of a notional navigation flight test program with specific requirements and performance specifications to be assessed. The students will present their results, analysis, conclusions, and recommendations to an audience composed of simulated stakeholders including the systems program office, contractor engineers, and operational testers.

SY 7110A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 7111L and SY 7112A under a shared description block.
SY 7110A.description: This is a 3-hour academic overview of the types of documentation and specifications common in software and networks mission systems testing. The MIL-STD-1760 interface and MIL-STD-1553 data bus protocol is introduced as well as other military and commercial data busses. This block introduces the various software development and test methodologies used by defense industry partners and the DoD for aircraft, space and cyber systems, and how requirements definition, development, and test approaches differ between legacy and more modern development frameworks. It focuses on how to create a software test plan that meets specification, performance, maintenance, and quality requirements while effectively managing the tradeoffs between risk, quality, and cost. Finally, the block introduces the DevSecOps cycle, Open Mission Systems fundamentals, and continuous integration and continuous development concepts as applied to software testing of aircraft and spacecraft systems. This block also discusses vehicles as a system of systems, then decomposes individual systems using the design process down to specific mission systems, mission essential functionality, and cyber-physical systems technologies. This provides a context necessary to understand how information flow within cyber-physical systems and physical technology shapes integrated avionics/regression testing as well as cyber vulnerability analysis. These labs reinforce academic knowledge acquired in the lecture with a 1-hour lab conducted with 4 students at a time. Students can manipulate code on a stand-alone computer to illustrate vulnerabilities such as buffer overflows and race conditions. They are also exposed to automated testing using CI/CD pipelines. Additionally, students demonstrate the performance differences between programming languages (compiled and interpreted), virtualization technologies (virtual machines and containers), Internet Protocol (IP) network data transfers (TCP and UDP), and cryptographic algorithms (AES and RSA) to better internalize the tradeoffs and promise of the cutting-edge computer science currently being used in modern mission systems architectures.

SY 7111L.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 7110A and SY 7112A under a shared description block.
SY 7111L.description: This is a 3-hour academic overview of the types of documentation and specifications common in software and networks mission systems testing. The MIL-STD-1760 interface and MIL-STD-1553 data bus protocol is introduced as well as other military and commercial data busses. This block introduces the various software development and test methodologies used by defense industry partners and the DoD for aircraft, space and cyber systems, and how requirements definition, development, and test approaches differ between legacy and more modern development frameworks. It focuses on how to create a software test plan that meets specification, performance, maintenance, and quality requirements while effectively managing the tradeoffs between risk, quality, and cost. Finally, the block introduces the DevSecOps cycle, Open Mission Systems fundamentals, and continuous integration and continuous development concepts as applied to software testing of aircraft and spacecraft systems. This block also discusses vehicles as a system of systems, then decomposes individual systems using the design process down to specific mission systems, mission essential functionality, and cyber-physical systems technologies. This provides a context necessary to understand how information flow within cyber-physical systems and physical technology shapes integrated avionics/regression testing as well as cyber vulnerability analysis. These labs reinforce academic knowledge acquired in the lecture with a 1-hour lab conducted with 4 students at a time. Students can manipulate code on a stand-alone computer to illustrate vulnerabilities such as buffer overflows and race conditions. They are also exposed to automated testing using CI/CD pipelines. Additionally, students demonstrate the performance differences between programming languages (compiled and interpreted), virtualization technologies (virtual machines and containers), Internet Protocol (IP) network data transfers (TCP and UDP), and cryptographic algorithms (AES and RSA) to better internalize the tradeoffs and promise of the cutting-edge computer science currently being used in modern mission systems architectures.

SY 7120A.eventNotes: SY 7401E prereq list cites this as 'SY 7120A Remotely Piloted Aircraft (RPA) and Autonomy'; MCG heading here is 'Testing Unmanned Platforms'.
SY 7120A.description: This 2-hour lecture is an introduction to the unique testing considerations for unmanned platforms from a mission systems point of view. Subjects covered will include RPA common classifications, system components and traits, and unique test considerations.

SY 7201A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 7202H under a shared description block.
SY 7201A.description: This 8-hour academic lecture course introduces students to the fundamental aspects of air-to-ground and air-to-air weapons and weapons integration testing. Topics include weapon components, guided weapons delivery testing, guidance modes, release point and impact point calculation, error budgets, safe separation, DT/OT teaming, some specialized weapons testing and instrumentation. The 2-hour field trip to the Edwards AFB weapons load hangar gives students hands-on experience with many of the weapons they may encounter during a test campaign.

SY 7202H.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 7201A under a shared description block.
SY 7202H.description: This 8-hour academic lecture course introduces students to the fundamental aspects of air-to-ground and air-to-air weapons and weapons integration testing. Topics include weapon components, guided weapons delivery testing, guidance modes, release point and impact point calculation, error budgets, safe separation, DT/OT teaming, some specialized weapons testing and instrumentation. The 2-hour field trip to the Edwards AFB weapons load hangar gives students hands-on experience with many of the weapons they may encounter during a test campaign.

SY 7210M.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 7211S, SY 7212F, SY 7213C, and SY 7214Y under a shared description block.
SY 7210M.description: Students will be placed into teams of 6-9 students to accomplish weapons regression and DLZ analysis and should be kept together for sims and flights to the maximum extent possible. For the simulator event, students will be issued flight test weapon cards to practice systems test conduct, as well as discuss system management, error propagation, and systems test safety. Student teams will practice test/control division of duties and responsibilities, while offering a first look at the F-16 weapons delivery event. The sortie will consist of three main areas: 1) Operational release of inert GBU-12 with self-lasing to a stationary target. 2) JDAM regression testing and DLZ analysis. 3) F-16 avionics regression testing. During the sortie, students will sharpen their applied weapons test conduct foundations and will be introduced to weapons telemetry in the control room. The academic concepts will be reinforced through system test/MCR conduct, LAR and accuracy testing strategies, multi-resource and multi-card test conduct practice, and system/weapons error recognition. CSO/P/RPA students will complete the simulator session prior to flight with ABM/FTEs. The students will work as a team to accomplish all desired test points for the JDAM DLZ analysis, balancing efficiency and effectiveness across multiple sorties. A 1-hour MIB outlines the flight and mission control room events.

SY 7220M.eventName: F-16 Weapons Delivery Domain Integration MIB

applicabilityDetail.description: No tag (applicability derived from paired events)

SY 7220M.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 7221W, SY 7222F, SY 7223C, and SY 7224Y under a shared description block.
SY 7220M.description: This sortie is designed to demonstrate close integration with the Space Test Course Systems Practical and give select students a broadening experience for working with agencies beyond their background domain. Pilots selected should be primarily those with an F-16 background (primary) or those with a background dropping GBU-12s (secondary). FTEs or CSOs selected should be those with previous control room experience or weapons delivery experience. FTC students will work closely with STC students to plan and execute a precision Time-On-Target GBU-12 weapon delivery to correspond with commercial satellite EO/IR pass. FTC students will also accomplish JDAM regression and DLZ analysis, along with any other regression cards in the time and fuel remaining. FTC students will assist STC students with any post-flight analysis. FTC and STC students will be scheduled for a 1-hour mission planning cell at minimum. Students will give an oral debrief of their JDAM DLZ error analysis as a group using relevant data collected during the F-16 flight (and sim if applicable).

applicabilityDetail.description: ABM/CSO/RPA mandatory; select FTE/P (FTE and pilots at instructor discretion)

SY 7501M.eventNotes: 'select FTE/P' is a special applicability — FTE and pilots at instructor discretion. Grouped in MCG with SY 7502S, SY 7503F, and SY 7504Y under a shared description block.
SY 7501M.description: This is an evaluation event for the ABM/CSO/RPA and select FTE/P students' Mission Systems curricula. The MIB outlines the practical exam requirements. Students are required to select appropriate targets for sensor and weapons testing, develop a mission profile and cards to accomplish a limited developmental test and evaluation of the MQ-9 sensors and ground weapons delivery system. Students receive an MQ-9 controls and displays orientation in a ground control segment (GCS) simulator. The mission phase is conducted using either a real MQ-9 or GCS simulator depending on aircraft availability. Students provide an expanded oral debrief of results after the mission.

Students are required to select appropriate targets for sensor and weapons testing, develop a mission profile and cards to accomplish a limited developmental test and evaluation of the MQ-9 sensors and ground weapons delivery system. Students receive an MQ-9 controls and displays orientation in a ground control segment (GCS) simulator. The mission phase is conducted using either a real MQ-9 or GCS simulator depending on aircraft availability. Students provide an expanded oral debrief of results after the mission.

SY 7520M.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 7521O and SY 7522Y under a shared description block.
SY 7520M.description: This practical will integrate the material presented in the mission systems platform course. Students will execute the exercise and complete a written report and oral presentation. Students will develop the test approach based on a scenario provided. Instructors will provide limited coaching and students will be expected to work in small teams to complete event. Multi-domain aspects of T&E will be considered. The project will integrate and reinforce the materials presented up to this point in the curricula and give the students an opportunity to execute a test project from beginning to end. Students will be tasked to evaluate a relevant space system. Students will be required to develop a test plan for review and approval. Students will then conduct the test using the assets available for the evaluation, analyze the data and draft a final report of the results. The scope of the exercise will be limited in order allow execution within the time constraints of the block.

SY 8101A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 8102E and SY 8103A under a shared description block.
SY 8101A.description: This 3-hour academic lecture introduces students to the fundamental concepts of communications and datalinks systems in modern aircraft. The course reviews the types of information that is communicated using communications technologies, introduces the basic technical performance measures shared by digital communications systems, fundamental test techniques used for all communications systems, and introduces datalink test fundamentals.

SY 8201A.description: This 2-hour academic lecture introduces data fusion models, methods, examples, and test techniques. The course builds on individual systems and sensors knowledge to teach how sensors can be integrated and fused into a user-friendly interface.

SY 8301A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 8302A under a shared description block.
SY 8301A.description: This 1-hour academic lecture will introduce students to the principles of advanced, network-enabled weapons test techniques.

SY 8302A.eventNotes: (US Only) — security restriction, not a student-type filter. Grouped in MCG with SY 8301A under a shared description block.
SY 8302A.description: This 1-hour academic lecture will introduce students to the principles of advanced, network-enabled weapons test techniques.

SY 8401A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with SY 8402A under a shared description block.
SY 8401A.description: This 7-hour academic lecture introduces the student to basics of Electromagnetic Warfare (EW) and EW test and evaluation. The course then builds on the RADAR and communications courses with respect to those electromagnetic properties that support information flow to various sensors and how EW techniques can be used to interfere with that information flow. The course introduces the concept of Integrated Air Defense Systems (IADS) and the "kill chain" used by both offensive and defensive weapons. The course continues with Electromagnetic Warfare and EW testing, followed by Low Observable (L-O) technology and L-O testing.

The course continues with Electromagnetic Warfare and EW testing, followed by Low Observable (L-O) technology and L-O testing.

TF 5101A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5101A, TF 5102A, TF 5103R, TF 5104Z under a shared description block.
TF 5101A.description: The first block will teach students the fundamentals of Systems Engineering and how it applies to test. It covers complexity, systems theory, the scientific method, an abstract approach for decomposing the modern battlespace into test requirements, and a process for generating data and reducing system risk. The following blocks will then build on these modeling concepts and provide simple constructs for modeling systems from function to form. The written report provides an opportunity for the student to engage individually in Systems Engineering artifact development and communicate their purpose and importance to complexity management, test strategy development, and risk management using a notional system provided by the instructor. This project consists of a short technical report on a notional complex system, providing an opportunity for the student to demonstrate comprehension across a spectrum of Systems Engineering artifacts.

TF 5201A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5201A, TF 5202S, TF 5203A under a shared description block.
TF 5201A.description: The first block introduces Human System Integration (HSI) as a systems-based scientific methodology and highlights test considerations. It presents considerations for the human element cross-over into other all other disciplines. This block will introduce concepts of cognitive workload assessment and conducting HSI evaluations of systems. This block will also introduce qualitative and quantitative approaches to Human Systems Integration. The Model-Based Human Systems Integration (MB HSI) sim/lab orients students to human systems testing in a simulation environment. During a 1.5-hour test block, each student will receive a simulator orientation and individual simulator tasks. Basic Performance Resource measures will be collected and used in appropriate curriculum events for analysis. The last block reviews aspects of the human role in the operations process and describes the role of the TPS graduate in the Human Systems testing process. The block emphasizes space/aviation Human Systems history as it relates to major aviation, military, and space developments. The topics covered include control and display principles, along with cognitive workload assessment. Additionally, the last block covers basic functionality and limitations of human sensation and perception, as well as components of situation awareness, models of decision making, and case studies of both, mishaps and recent real-world test programs involving Human Machine Interface.

TF 5210L.description: The objective of this lab is to introduce the student to the science of Anthropometry (the measurement of human body dimensions). Anthropometric technicians will measure a variety of body dimensions for each student. Those dimensions will be placed into a Human Systems computer database which describes how students' individual body dimensions compare to the historic US Air Force aircrew population. Each student will be provided a copy of their anthropometric measurements and percentiles, which can be used for future evaluations both at TPS and follow-on test assignments.

TF 5230M.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5230M, TF 5231S, TF 5232R under a shared description block. MCG prereq line cites 'TF 6430A Deficiency Reports Lecture' (event type A) though the canonical event is TF 6430B; preserved verbatim as written.
TF 5230M.description: The purpose of the MIB is to orient students to the scope and expectations of the Human Systems Evaluation Modified Technical Report and provide a dedicated opportunity for students to query the instructor regarding details of the report. Expectations for the system under test, data collection methods, and design of experiments guidelines will be provided. Students will work in groups to collect task performance data, evaluate the system interface, and provide design change recommendations in the technical report.

TF 5231S.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5230M, TF 5231S, TF 5232R under a shared description block. MCG prereq line cites 'TF 6430A Deficiency Reports Lecture' (event type A) though the canonical event is TF 6430B; preserved verbatim as written.
TF 5231S.description: The purpose of the MIB is to orient students to the scope and expectations of the Human Systems Evaluation Modified Technical Report and provide a dedicated opportunity for students to query the instructor regarding details of the report. Expectations for the system under test, data collection methods, and design of experiments guidelines will be provided. Students will work in groups to collect task performance data, evaluate the system interface, and provide design change recommendations in the technical report.

TF 5232R.eventName: Human Systems Evaluation Modified Technical Report
TF 5232R.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5230M, TF 5231S, TF 5232R under a shared description block. MCG prereq line cites 'TF 6430A Deficiency Reports Lecture' (event type A) though the canonical event is TF 6430B; preserved verbatim as written.

TF 5310M.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5310M, TF 5311Y under a shared description block.
TF 5310M.description: Students will conduct independent study using provided policy documents (DoD, DAF, and USSF instructions and guidance) related to Test and Evaluation. Students will review, research, and present relevant information to their classmates and instructors with a focus on their role in an Integrated Test Team (ITT). Each student will be assigned a single document to research and work with a group to develop a 45-minute oral presentation on the assigned document.

TF 5311Y.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5310M, TF 5311Y under a shared description block.
TF 5311Y.description: Students will conduct independent study using provided policy documents (DoD, DAF, and USSF instructions and guidance) related to Test and Evaluation. Students will review, research, and present relevant information to their classmates and instructors with a focus on their role in an Integrated Test Team (ITT). Each student will be assigned a single document to research and work with a group to develop a 45-minute oral presentation on the assigned document.

TF 5320A.eventName: Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP) Development and Strategic Planning (STC)
TF 5320A.eventNotes: MCG prereq line cites 'TF 5310Y Test Policy Oral Report (STC)' though the canonical event is TF 5311Y; preserved verbatim. MCG prereq also cites 'TF 5301A Introduction to Space Systems Testing (STC)' which does not appear as a defined event in this phase; preserved verbatim. Prereq citation "TF 5310Y Test Policy Oral Report (STC)" corrected to "TF 5311Y" per user ruling (MCG source typo; 5310/5311 sequence confirmed by adjacent events).
TF 5320A.description: Students will be introduced to DAF and OSD guidance on development of test strategies and TEMPs. This course will consist of a lecture, guided research using OSD or DAF-provided tools, case studies, and a student project. Given actual acquisition program documents, students will review a TEMP from the perspective of an oversight organization.

TF 5401A.description: This block introduces the framework which can be applied to complex systems and used for risk and safety analysis. This framework will be used throughout the curriculum for modeling and assessing a range of systems from simple to complex.

TF 5410A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5410A, TF 5411A, TF 5412A, TF 5413A under a shared description block.
TF 5410A.description: These blocks provide the basic knowledge of test safety and hazard/risk identification, to include safety planning, Final Safety Review (FSR), test plan approval, test execution, and the project test plan change process. Test safety processes will be reinforced through practical exercises throughout the course. Students receive initial Project Safety Lead (PSL) qualifications upon completion of this course.

TF 5411A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5410A, TF 5411A, TF 5412A, TF 5413A under a shared description block.
TF 5411A.description: These blocks provide the basic knowledge of test safety and hazard/risk identification, to include safety planning, Final Safety Review (FSR), test plan approval, test execution, and the project test plan change process. Test safety processes will be reinforced through practical exercises throughout the course. Students receive initial Project Safety Lead (PSL) qualifications upon completion of this course.

TF 5412A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5410A, TF 5411A, TF 5412A, TF 5413A under a shared description block.
TF 5412A.description: These blocks provide the basic knowledge of test safety and hazard/risk identification, to include safety planning, Final Safety Review (FSR), test plan approval, test execution, and the project test plan change process. Test safety processes will be reinforced through practical exercises throughout the course. Students receive initial Project Safety Lead (PSL) qualifications upon completion of this course.

TF 5413A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5410A, TF 5411A, TF 5412A, TF 5413A under a shared description block.
TF 5413A.description: These blocks provide the basic knowledge of test safety and hazard/risk identification, to include safety planning, Final Safety Review (FSR), test plan approval, test execution, and the project test plan change process. Test safety processes will be reinforced through practical exercises throughout the course. Students receive initial Project Safety Lead (PSL) qualifications upon completion of this course.

TF 5420A.description: This course presents the Functional Mission Analysis, a hazard analysis technique based on an extended model of mishap causation. Component failures, unsafe interactions, and social-technical systems − or the combination of all three − can be analyzed as a whole. Case studies of applications to past mishaps are presented. Students are led through applications to posed test projects to practice Functional Mission Analysis application.

TF 5501M.eventName: Photo/Safety Chase Introduction and Familiarization MIB (ABM/CSO/P/FTE)
TF 5501M.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5502F, TF 5503F under a shared description block.
TF 5501M.description: The objective of this mission is to familiarize the student with differences in photo, safety, and area chase. Students will practice or observe roles and responsibilities for a representative variety of chase maneuvers from both the test aircraft and the chase aircraft. These roles include test/chase communication and coordination, in the test role; precise flight test execution and management of chase, and in the chase role; flying techniques for a variety of chase challenges.

applicabilityDetail.description: ABM, CSO, FTE — T-38 Photo/Safety Chase Familiarization

TF 5510M.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 5511F, TF 5520F under a shared description block.
TF 5510M.description: The objective of this mission is for RPA student pilots to understand and apply procedures and techniques for 2-ship maneuvering and photo/safety chase for UAV chase missions. Student pilots have the optional opportunity to fly as target.

TF 5520F.description: The objective of this mission is for RPA student pilots to understand and apply procedures and techniques for 2-ship maneuvering and photo/safety chase for UAV chase missions. Student pilots have the optional opportunity to fly as target.

TF 6101A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6101A.eventNotes: Grouped in MCG with TF 6101A, TF 6102A, TF 6103Y, TF 6104A under a shared description block.
TF 6101A.description: The first block introduces students to the basic components involved with planning, executing, analyzing, and reporting during a test program. It provides context for the TF 6000 level of academics and introduces some of the basic "vocabulary" of aerospace test programs. The second block introduces students to the responsibilities of a tester, provides the context to help them understand where developmental testing fits into the greater acquisition enterprise, and introduces them to the processes which support test planning and execution. The third block will introduce students to the basic format and development strategy for Air Force Test Center (AFTC)-style test plans. It will explain the construct of specific and achievable objectives and detail the sub-sections that are hallmarks of an executable plan that will answer the customer's questions. The oral report consists of a ten-minute group presentation that will take place a few days after the Test Planning Lecture. Students will analyze a provided test vignette, identify requirements and test objectives, develop a test methodology, identify a risk, and communicate their plan.
